source_paper_title: Popular Maximum-Utility Matchings with Matroid Constraints

For the one-sided preferences model, we demonstrate that this problem is as hard as the exact matching problem [46] (see Section 5.1 for the definition), for which the existence of a deterministic efficient algorithm remains a longstanding open question.
title: Deterministic polynomial-time algorithm for exact matching in bipartite graphs
background_latex:
Let \(G=(A,B;E)\) be a bipartite graph in which every edge is colored either red or blue, and let \(|A|=|B|\). A matching \(M\subseteq E\) is perfect if every vertex in \(A\cup B\) is incident to exactly one edge of \(M\). For a perfect matching \(M\), let \(r(M)\) denote the number of red edges in \(M\).

The \(\textit{exact matching problem}\) asks, given \((G,k)\) with integer \(k\), whether there exists a perfect matching \(M\) with \(r(M)=k\). The paper studies a different class of problems (popular near-maximum-weight matchings) and proves that if one could solve a certain popularity-constrained matching problem deterministically in polynomial time, then one could solve the exact matching problem deterministically in polynomial time. The paper notes that no deterministic polynomial-time algorithm is known for exact matching.
problem_definition_latex: Design a deterministic polynomial-time algorithm that, given a bipartite graph \(G=(A,B;E)\) with red/blue edge colors and an integer \(k\), decides whether there exists a perfect matching \(M\subseteq E\) such that \(r(M)=k\) (i.e., \(M\) contains exactly \(k\) red edges), and outputs such an \(M\) when it exists.
latex:
Let \(G=(A,B;E)\) be a bipartite graph in which every edge is colored either red or blue, and let \(|A|=|B|\). A matching \(M\subseteq E\) is perfect if every vertex in \(A\cup B\) is incident to exactly one edge of \(M\). For a perfect matching \(M\), let \(r(M)\) denote the number of red edges in \(M\).

The \(\textit{exact matching problem}\) asks, given \((G,k)\) with integer \(k\), whether there exists a perfect matching \(M\) with \(r(M)=k\). The paper studies a different class of problems (popular near-maximum-weight matchings) and proves that if one could solve a certain popularity-constrained matching problem deterministically in polynomial time, then one could solve the exact matching problem deterministically in polynomial time. The paper notes that no deterministic polynomial-time algorithm is known for exact matching.

Design a deterministic polynomial-time algorithm that, given a bipartite graph \(G=(A,B;E)\) with red/blue edge colors and an integer \(k\), decides whether there exists a perfect matching \(M\subseteq E\) such that \(r(M)=k\) (i.e., \(M\) contains exactly \(k\) red edges), and outputs such an \(M\) when it exists.
location_in_paper: Page 6 (Introduction, discussion before Theorem 4) and page 22 (Section 5.1, statement of Theorem 4 and surrounding text).
open_problem_quote: For the one-sided preferences model, we demonstrate that this problem is as hard as the exact matching problem [46] (see Section 5.1 for the definition), for which the existence of a deterministic efficient algorithm remains a longstanding open question.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The statement is an algorithm-existence question over all bipartite graphs rather than a falsifiable universal inequality; there is no finite counterexample to search for. Instance generation can test heuristics but cannot refute existence of an algorithm.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: An evolutionary coding loop could search for practical deterministic algorithms/heuristics and evaluate them on many random and adversarial instances with a verifiable oracle (e.g., via randomized isolation or exponential verification for small sizes). However, demonstrating a true polynomial-time algorithm with correctness for all instances requires proofs beyond empirical fitness.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The exact matching problem of Papadimitriou–Yannakakis asks for a perfect matching with exactly \(k\) red edges in a red/blue-colored bipartite graph \(G=(A,B;E)\). As emphasized in Csáji–Király–Takazawa–Yokoi (2024), this problem remains a central derandomization challenge: randomized polynomial-time algorithms are known (via the isolation lemma/Tutte matrix style techniques), but a deterministic polynomial-time algorithm is still not known in general.

The 2024 paper uses exact matching as a hardness yardstick for “popular near-maximum(-weight)” matching: Theorem 4 shows that a deterministic polynomial-time algorithm for the one-sided popular near-maximum-weight matching problem (even with weights in \(\{0,1\}\) and weak orders) would imply a deterministic polynomial-time algorithm for exact matching. Thus, their tractability results for popular maximum-weight/maximum-utility solutions (Theorems 1–3) are complemented by evidence that moving from “optimal” to “near-optimal” popularity constraints runs into the same barrier as exact matching.

Among the forward citations provided, “Max-Utility Matchings with Popularity via Critical Vertices” develops deterministic polynomial-time algorithms and polyhedral formulations for several popularity-with-utility variants (popular critical matchings, popular max-utility matchings). While methodologically adjacent (it also leverages structure of popular matchings and criticality), it does not address the red/blue exact-count constraint and does not yield a deterministic algorithm for exact matching. Consequently, based on the available forward-citation evidence, the exact matching problem remains open, and progress continues to be indirect—either by reductions showing that new deterministic algorithms would derandomize exact matching, or by developing deterministic algorithms for neighboring constrained-popularity problems that carefully avoid exact-count constraints.

sources.title: Max-Utility Matchings with Popularity via Critical Vertices
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper proves existence and gives polynomial-time algorithms for popularity notions in bipartite matching with strict preferences and edge utilities. In particular: (i) for any critical set \(C\subseteq A\cup B\), a popular critical matching exists and a max-size (and min-size) popular critical matching can be computed in \(O(m+|C|m)\) time (Theorem 1); (ii) for any utility function \(f:E\to\mathbb{Q}\), a popular max-utility matching exists and can be computed in polynomial time (Theorem 2); (iii) the popular max-critical matching polytope admits a compact extended formulation (Theorem 3).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for a deterministic polynomial-time algorithm for the exact matching problem: given a red/blue-colored bipartite graph and \(k\), decide if there is a perfect matching with exactly \(k\) red edges. This paper studies a different framework (popular matchings under preferences, critical vertices, and utilities) and provides deterministic polytime algorithms there. However, nothing in the provided excerpt indicates a reduction that would yield a deterministic polytime algorithm for exact matching, nor does it address counting red edges in perfect matchings. At best, the paper supplies algorithmic/LP machinery for certain constrained popularity/utility matching problems, which is conceptually adjacent to the original paper’s theme but does not resolve exact matching.
